   • In recent years US venture-backed deal count has been roughly half of global deals (Crunchbase reports ~45–55% share).  
   • PitchBook data shows US companies receive ~60% of total deal value, but number of companies is lower.  
3. Data sources/methods:  
   • Crunchbase, PitchBook, CB Insights—sampling all recorded VC rounds.  
   • Government filings (SEC for US deals).  
   • Surveys of VC firms (global allocation).

   • Size and maturity of US VC ecosystem.  
   • Growth of VC markets in China, India, Europe.  
   • Currency fluctuations affecting deal value but less so count.  
   • Industry concentration (tech hubs vs. emerging markets).  
5. Potential biases/limitations:  
   • Database coverage skews to US firms and English-language sources.  
   • Underreporting of deals in emerging markets.  
   • Variation in definition of “venture-backed.”  
6. Assumptions:  
   • Coverage bias moderately correctable; global non-US deals are under-captured by ~10–20%.  
   • Definition of “based in the US” is by HQ location at time of funding.  
7. Temporal/subgroup changes:  
   • US share peaked around 2018–2021; may be slightly declining as Asia grows.  
   • Early-stage deals more US-concentrated than later stages.  
8. Quantile estimates (percent of all VC-backed companies based in US):  
   • 5th percentile ~35%  
   • 25th percentile ~45%  
   • 50th percentile (median) ~55%  
   • 75th percentile ~65%  
   • 95th percentile ~75%  
9. Relevant context:  
   • US hosts major VC hubs (Silicon Valley, NYC, Boston).  
   • Asia (China, India) and Europe growing but still behind in deal count.  
10. Justification of choices:  
   • Median aligned with observed ~50–55% share.  
   • Spread reflects uncertainty from data biases and evolving markets.  
11. Focus on parameter uncertainty:  
   • We express uncertainty about the true global proportion, not variability in individual deal outcomes.  
12. Interpretation of spread:  
   • A ~40-point interquantile range indicates moderate uncertainty but confidence that US share is neither extremely low (<35%) nor extremely high (>75%).  
13. Edge cases/alternative scenarios:  
   • If emerging-market reporting dramatically improves, true US share could be at lower end (~35%).  
   • If US remains dominant in startup formation, could be at upper end (~75%).  
</parameter_estimation_process>

Based on multiple industry data sources indicating US-based deals comprise roughly half of global VC-backed company counts—and accounting for underreporting outside the US and growth trends in Asia and Europe—I center my median at 55%. A 40-point range between the 5th and 95th percentiles captures uncertainty from data coverage biases and market evolution.

<confidence_level>
Moderate confidence. I rely on industry aggregates but recognize coverage bias and changing global VC dynamics, hence a fairly wide uncertainty range.

<parameter_estimation_process>

   • Population mean (µ) of “number of employees” across all venture-backed companies.

2. Known facts / data points  
   • Many early-stage (Seed/Series A) startups: ~10–30 employees.  
   • Mid-stage (Series B/C): ~50–150 employees.  
   • Late-stage / unicorns: 200–1,000+ employees (but few in number).  
   • Crunchbase/PitchBook report median headcount often <50, but mean is pulled up by larger firms.

   • Crunchbase or PitchBook API: aggregate “employee_count” over all funded companies.  
   • NVCA Yearbook / CB Insights: summary statistics by funding round.  
   • SEC filings or LinkedIn headcount snapshots.  
   • Bootstrapped samples of company-level employee counts.

4. Factors influencing the mean  
   • Distribution of companies by funding stage (higher-stage companies have more employees).  
   • Industry mix (biotech R&D vs. software, hardware, consumer).  
   • Geography (Silicon Valley vs. emerging markets).  
   • Vintage year (younger vs. older cohorts).

5. Potential biases / limitations  
   • Survivorship bias: defunct or acquisitional exits may drop out of public datasets.  
   • Reporting bias: self-reported employee counts may lag.  
   • Coverage bias: some small or stealth startups never report headcount.

   • “Venture-backed” includes Seed through late-stage (pre-IPO) rounds.  
   • Each company counted once, regardless of current operational status (alive or closed).  
   • Data from the last 2–3 years is representative of the current population.

   • Bio/healthcare startups average fewer employees early (R&D labs) but grow large later.  
   • Software startups scale headcount more slowly until product–market fit.

   • The high end (around 90) allows that more mid/late-stage firms (or undercounting small ones) would raise it.  
   • The median of our belief distribution is set by most plausible current industry mix (~60).

   • These percentiles express my uncertainty about the true average headcount across all venture-backed companies, not the variance of individual company sizes.

12. What the spread indicates  
   • A ~60-point inter-centile range (5th to 95th) reflects moderate uncertainty driven by composition and data quality issues.

   • If a dataset disproportionately captures unicorns, µ could exceed 100.  
   • If many very small pre-seed firms were included, µ could drop toward 20–30.

1. Parameter type:
   - Estimating the population mean (average total funding raised in millions USD) for U.S.-based, venture-backed technology companies with at most 8 employees.

   - Early-stage rounds (pre-seed to seed) typically range from \$0.2 M to \$3 M.
   - A smaller subset of startups raise larger seed or small Series A rounds (\$4 M–\$8 M).
   - Many micro-startups raise under \$1 M via convertible notes or angels.

3. Possible data sources/methods:
   - Crunchbase, PitchBook, AngelList aggregated funding data.
   - NVCA year-end reports on seed and Series A averages.

   - Subsector (SaaS vs. deep tech vs. consumer apps).
   - Geographic clustering (SF Bay vs. Midwest).
   - Lead investor check size norms.

5. Potential biases/limitations:
   - Survivorship bias: publicly reported startups tend to have higher raises.
   - Reporting bias: seed rounds often under-disclosed.
   - Sample skew: databases overrepresent “success stories.”

6. Assumptions:
   - Database sample roughly representative of all VC-backed early tech startups.
   - Funding environment stable over the last 2–3 years for estimation.
   - “Total raised” equals cumulative pre-Series A funding.

7. Temporal/subgroup considerations:
   - Round amounts have trended upward in recent years.
   - Companies at 7–8 employees may have done larger seed or small Series A.

8. Quantile estimates (population mean):
   - 5th percentile ~0.3 M: in a scenario where most raises were tiny convertible notes.
   - 25th percentile ~1.0 M: lower-end seed rounds dominate.
   - 50th percentile ~2.0 M: typical seed funding for many small tech startups.
   - 75th percentile ~3.5 M: more startups raising larger seed or Series A crossing the 8-employee threshold.
   - 95th percentile ~6.0 M: rare cases of outsized early rounds pulling the mean upward.

9. Relevant context:
   - “At most 8 employees” captures truly early teams; larger rounds often accompany growth beyond this headcount.
   - Post-2020 fundraising environment has been enriched by record dry-powder at seed investors.

10. Justification of choices:
    - Centered around common seed ranges (\$1 M–\$3 M).
    - Tail values reflect both very small convertible note raises and occasional larger pre-A rounds.

11. Emphasis on parameter uncertainty:
    - We are uncertain about the true population mean; the spread between the 5th and 95th percentiles reflects this.

12. Reflection on estimate spread:
    - A roughly \$5.7 M inter-percentile range indicates moderate uncertainty given limited public data.

13. Edge cases/alternatives:
    - In a more conservative funding climate, all quantiles would shift downward by ~20–30%.
    - In hyper-inflated markets, the upper quantiles could double.
